original_hypothesis.title: ERC20Vault onMessageRecalled Token-ETH Coupling Causes Permanent Fund Lock
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: In ERC20Vault.onMessageRecalled, _transferTokens() is called first, then _message.srcOwner.sendEther(_message.value). If the canonical token is paused, blacklisted, or the vault has insufficient balance (due to a separate issue), _transferTokens reverts, preventing the ETH refund as well. The entire recall atomically fails, locking both the user's tokens AND ETH in the bridge/vault with no recovery path. The message status is already RECALLED-in-progress, and the recall cannot be retried.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The atomic coupling of token transfer and ETH refund in onMessageRecalled means a failure in either operation blocks both. Since recall is triggered from recallMessage which handles the status transition, a reverting callback prevents the status from reaching RECALLED, keeping it at NEW. But the proofReceipt is already set, so timing conditions apply on re-attempt.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _transferTokens called at line 237 before sendEther at line 238; if _transferTokens reverts, ETH refund is blocked

preconditions: Message was sent with both ERC20 tokens and ETH value
Message failed on destination chain (FAILED status)
Canonical token becomes non-transferable (paused/blacklisted) before recall executes
Message's from address supports IRecallableSender (for vault callback)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: _transferTokens(ctoken, srcOwner, amount) then srcOwner.sendEther(value) — atomic failure blocks both
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: If canonical token is paused by its admin after bridge send but before recall, both tokens and ETH are locked
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Not a direct profit attack but creates permanent fund lock for bridge users

economic_questions: Can the recall be retried after the token becomes transferable again?
Does the proofReceipt state allow multiple recall attempts?
Is the ETH value permanently locked or can it be recovered through another path?
validation_requirements: Test recall failure when token is paused and verify if retry is possible
Analyze recallMessage state after onMessageRecalled callback reverts
Quantify potential locked funds from common pausable tokens

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: None for attacker (exploits external token pause)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Locked funds per affected message (tokens + ETH)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: 100% of bridge message value if permanently locked
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Permanent lock if token pause is permanent

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After bridge send, before recall execution, during token pause event
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Potentially significant if popular pausable tokens are bridged

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users who bridged pausable ERC20 tokens with ETH value
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All in-flight bridge messages using pausable tokens

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: List of commonly bridged tokens with pause capabilities
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify recallMessage behavior when onMessageRecalled callback reverts

validation_result.mechanism: atomic token-ETH coupling in recall path
validation_result.impact_summary: ERC20Vault.onMessageRecalled atomically couples token transfer and ETH refund. If _transferTokens reverts (paused/blacklisted canonical token), the ETH refund is also blocked, locking user ETH for the duration of the token's non-transferability.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The hypothesis identifies a real code defect in ERC20Vault.onMessageRecalled where _transferTokens is called before sendEther. If _transferTokens reverts (e.g., the canonical token is paused by its admin), the entire onMessageRecalled callback reverts. Since this callback is invoked by Bridge.recallMessage, the whole recallMessage transaction reverts, reverting both the status change to RECALLED and the proofReceipt deletion. The message status remains NEW, and the proofReceipt remains set.

Critically, the hypothesis is partially incorrect about the retry behavior. Because the revert undoes the status change, messageStatus[msgHash] remains NEW, and proofReceipt[msgHash].receivedAt remains non-zero. This means recallMessage CAN be called again without re-proving. On retry, isMessageProven will be true (receivedAt != 0), and if the invocation delay has passed, it will attempt the callback again. If the token is still paused, it will fail again.

The core defect is that ETH and token recovery are unnecessarily coupled. The ETH (which is held by the Bridge, not the vault) could theoretically be refunded independently, but the code provides no mechanism to do so. If the canonical token is permanently paused or the vault's balance is depleted, the ETH is permanently locked alongside the tokens.

The same pattern exists in ERC721Vault.onMessageRecalled and ERC1155Vault.onMessageRecalled.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal is confirmed. In ERC20Vault.sol's onMessageRecalled function, _transferTokens is called before _message.srcOwner.sendEther(_message.value). In Bridge.sol's recallMessage, the status is set to RECALLED and proofReceipt is deleted BEFORE the callback is invoked, but since it's all in one transaction, a revert in the callback undoes everything. The pattern is verified across all three vault types.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: _transferTokens is called before sendEther. If _transferTokens reverts (canonical token paused, blacklisted, or insufficient vault balance), the ETH refund via sendEther is also blocked. The entire callback reverts, causing Bridge.recallMessage to revert as well.

When ctoken.chainId == block.chainid (canonical token on this chain), safeTransfer is called which will revert if the token is paused, the recipient is blacklisted, or the vault has insufficient balance. This revert propagates up and blocks the ETH refund.

The Bridge sets status to RECALLED and deletes proofReceipt BEFORE invoking the callback. If the callback (onMessageRecalled on the vault) reverts, the entire transaction reverts, meaning status remains NEW and proofReceipt is NOT deleted. While this allows retries, it also means a persistently failing token transfer permanently blocks the ETH refund.

calling_sequence: 1. User calls Bridge.recallMessage with proof of failure
2. Bridge verifies message status is NEW and proof is valid
3. After invocation delay, Bridge deletes proofReceipt and sets status to RECALLED
9. If token is paused/blacklisted, safeTransfer REVERTS
11. All state changes (status = RECALLED, delete proofReceipt) are REVERTED
12. Message remains in NEW state with proofReceipt intact
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: Bridge.recallMessage -> IRecallableSender(_message.from).onMessageRecalled{value} -> ERC20Vault.onMessageRecalled -> _transferTokens -> IERC20.safeTransfer (REVERTS if token paused) -> entire tx reverts. ETH value that was sent with the callback is returned to Bridge. The proof receipt remains valid for retry, but retry will also fail if the token remains non-transferable. The ETH is locked in the Bridge contract with no independent recovery path.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment-specific configuration affects this vulnerability. The defect exists in the code logic regardless of deployment parameters.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Scenario: (1) User bridges 1000 USDC (pausable token) + 1 ETH via ERC20Vault.sendToken. (2) Message fails on destination or user initiates recall. (3) USDC admin pauses the token (independent action, could be in response to a hack or regulatory action). (4) User calls recallMessage - _transferTokens attempts IERC20(USDC).safeTransfer which reverts because USDC is paused. (5) The entire recallMessage reverts, ETH remains locked in Bridge. (6) User can retry recallMessage, but it will fail every time USDC is paused. (7) If USDC pause is permanent, both 1000 USDC and 1 ETH are permanently locked. This is not an attack per se, but a code defect that creates unnecessary fund lock risk. An adversarial token admin could deliberately pause a token to grief bridge users.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH severity. Any user who bridges a pausable/blacklistable canonical ERC20 token alongside ETH value risks having their ETH locked if the token becomes non-transferable. Real-world pausable tokens include USDC, USDT, and many other major tokens. The lock duration is at minimum equal to the token's pause duration, and potentially permanent. The same issue affects ERC721Vault and ERC1155Vault. While retry is possible (mitigating the 'permanent lock' claim somewhat), there is genuinely no mechanism to independently recover the ETH.
recommended_mitigations: Separate the token transfer and ETH refund in onMessageRecalled using try/catch: attempt _transferTokens in a try block, and always execute sendEther regardless of token transfer success
Add a separate fallback function that allows srcOwner to claim ETH independently if _transferTokens has been failing
Reverse the order: call sendEther before _transferTokens (ETH refund is less likely to fail than arbitrary token transfer)
Implement a two-step recall: first refund ETH, then allow token claim separately
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy Bridge, SignalService, ERC20Vault on two test chains. 2. Deploy a pausable ERC20 token (e.g., OpenZeppelin ERC20Pausable). 3. Bridge tokens + ETH via sendToken. 4. Force message failure on destination chain. 5. Pause the ERC20 token on source chain. 6. Attempt recallMessage - verify it reverts. 7. Verify ETH remains locked in Bridge. 8. Unpause token, verify recall now succeeds.
uncertainties: The hypothesis incorrectly states the message status gets stuck at 'RECALLED-in-progress' - it actually remains at NEW, allowing retries
The practical impact depends on whether commonly bridged tokens have permanent pause capabilities vs temporary pauses
The same issue exists in onMessageInvocation (destination side) where token mint failure blocks ETH delivery, though this results in RETRIABLE status
additional_testing_needed: Test with USDC adapter path to see if the same issue applies when USDCAdapter is the bridged token
Verify behavior when vault has insufficient balance of canonical token (separate from pause scenario)
Test the retry path after unpausing to confirm recovery works when the blocking condition is resolved
Test with ERC721 and ERC1155 vaults for the same pattern
